clinical_rationale.summary: The provided abstracts address advanced and quantitative MRI techniques for Multiple Sclerosis (MS) brain imaging, including ultrashort echo time myelin imaging (STAIR-UTE at 3T), quantitative T1 mapping for microstructural assessment, sodium MRI at 7T, and ultrahigh-field 7T neuroimaging broadly. None of the five abstracts provide parameter-dense (TE/TR explicit) data for a standard clinical MS brain protocol. The gold-standard journal evidence (Radiology, AJNR, RadioGraphics, European Radiology, Investigative Radiology) is focused on advanced/quantitative sequences rather than conventional clinical MS protocol parameters. Standard clinical MS brain MRI protocol parameters (FLAIR, T2, T1 pre/post, DWI, PDWI) are therefore synthesized primarily from established clinical knowledge and consensus guidelines (MAGNIMS, North American Imaging in MS), with the literature corroborating the importance of FLAIR for lesion detection and T1 mapping for microstructural characterization.
clinical_rationale.key_changes: Recent literature demonstrates a clear shift toward quantitative and advanced sequences in MS research imaging: STAIR-UTE enables direct myelin proton density imaging beyond conventional FLAIR; quantitative T1 mapping (variable flip angle) is increasingly validated as a biomarker of disability-relevant microstructural damage in normal-appearing white matter and grey matter in early RRMS; and sodium MRI at 7T reveals sodium concentration changes in MS subtypes sensitive to myelin loss. At 7T, superior lesion detection and cortical lesion conspicuity are well-documented. For routine clinical MS brain MRI, the MAGNIMS-CMSC consensus remains the backbone: 3D FLAIR is the primary lesion-detection sequence, supplemented by 3D T1-weighted (pre and post-gadolinium), DWI, and optionally PD/T2. No new consensus has displaced these standard sequences for routine 1.5T/3T clinical use, but the literature increasingly supports adding quantitative T1 mapping and potentially advanced myelin-sensitive sequences as the field matures toward clinical translation.
